en.extract:
Przevalski's finch (Urocynchramus pylzowi), Przewalski's finch or Przevalski's pinktail, is an unusual passerine bird endemic to the mountains of central-west China. The species is named after Mikhail Pyltsov, the Russian explorer who accompanied Nikolai Przhevalsky on the expedition in which specimens of the bird were collected. Its taxonomic affinities were unclear for a long time, giving rise to other common names, the pink-tailed bunting and the Przewalski's rosefinch. In 2000 it was proposed that it should in fact be regarded neither as a finch nor a bunting, but as the only member of the family Urocynchramidae, something that had been originally proposed in the German ornithological literature as long ago as 1918 by Janusz von Domaniewski, and also by Wolters in 1979. This change was adopted in the sixth edition of the Clements checklist.


Description and ecology
Przewalski's finch is a small bird similar in appearance to the long-tailed rosefinch. The tail is long and – quite unlike in typical finches – graduated, with the outer feathers much shorter than the central ones. The sexes are dimorphic, with the males having bright pink on the throat, breast and belly. Both sexes have brown streaked plumage on the back and wings. The bill is thinner than those of the rosefinches. The morphological feature which is diagnostic for the Urocynchramidae is the outer primary; in finches and buntings this feather is vestigial but in the Przewalski's finch it is two-thirds the length of the next primary.
This bird lives at elevations between 3,050 and 5,000 metres (10,010 and 16,400 ft), usually in pairs during the breeding season and in small flocks during the winter. Przhevalsky described the species' song as similar to that of buntings. The species has not been studied much in the wild, and little is known of its behaviour. It is not thought to be threatened by human activities and is listed as least concern on the IUCN Red List.


References


External links

 Data related to Przevalski's finch at Wikispecies
Pink-tailed Bunting Don Roberson; Bird Families of the World

'''Przevalski's finch''' ('''''Urocynchramus pylzowi'''''), '''Przewalski's finch''' or '''Przevalski's pinktail''', is an unusual [[passerine]] [[bird]] [[Endemism|endemic]] to the mountains of central-west [[China]]. The species is named after [[Mikhail Pyltsov]], the [[Russia]]n explorer who accompanied [[Nikolai Przhevalsky]] on the expedition in which specimens of the bird were collected. Its [[Taxonomy (biology)|taxonomic]] affinities were unclear for a long time, giving rise to other common names, the '''pink-tailed bunting''' and the '''Przewalski's rosefinch'''. In 2000 it was proposed that it should in fact be regarded neither as a [[finch]] nor a [[Emberiza|bunting]], but as the [[monotypic|only member]] of the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] '''Urocynchramidae''',<ref>{{cite journal|last=Groth|first=J.|year=2000|url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v117n03/p00787-p00791.pdf|title=Molecular evidence for the systematic position of ''Urocynchramus pylzowi''|journal=[[The Auk]]|volume=117|issue=3|pages= 787–792|doi= 10.1642/0004-8038(2000)117[0787:MEFTSP]2.0.CO;2|jstor=4089604|issn=0004-8038}}</ref> something that had been originally proposed in the German ornithological literature as long ago as 1918 by [[Janusz von Domaniewski]],<ref name=Domaniewski>{{cite journal|journal=Journal für Ornithologie|volume= 66|pages= 421–424|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/11806767|title=Die Stellung des ''Urocynchramus pylzowi'' Przev. in der Systematik|last=von Domaniewski|first=Janusz|year=1918|language=German|doi=10.1007/BF02251733|issue=4|s2cid= 13047981}}</ref> and also by Wolters in 1979. This change was adopted in the sixth edition of the [[Clements checklist]].<ref>{{cite book|last=Clements|first= J. F.|year=2007|title=The Clements Checklist of Birds of the World| isbn =978-0-8014-4501-9}}</ref>

==Description and ecology==
Przewalski's finch is a small bird similar in appearance to the [[long-tailed rosefinch]]. The tail is long and – quite unlike in typical finches – graduated, with the outer feathers much shorter than the central ones. The sexes are [[sexual dimorphism|dimorphic]], with the males having bright pink on the throat, breast and belly. Both sexes have brown streaked [[plumage]] on the back and wings. The [[beak|bill]] is thinner than those of the rosefinches.{{Citation needed|date=December 2011}} The morphological feature which is diagnostic for the Urocynchramidae is the outer [[primary feather|primary]]; in finches and buntings this feather is [[vestigial]] but in the Przewalski's finch it is two-thirds the length of the next primary.<ref name=Domaniewski/>

This bird lives at elevations between {{convert|3050|and|5000|m|ft}}, usually in pairs during the breeding season and in small flocks during the winter. Przhevalsky described the species' song as similar to that of buntings.{{Citation needed|date=December 2011}} The species has not been studied much in the wild, and little is known of its behaviour. It is not thought to be threatened by human activities and is listed as [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

zh.extract:
朱鹀（学名：Urocynchramus pylzowi）为朱鹀科朱鹀属下唯一的鸟类种类，是中国的特有物种。分布于甘肃、青海、四川等地，主要生活于一般在海拔500-2500m 高处以及栖息于山谷和山溪两岸的矮柳丛和小型灌丛中。该物种的模式产地在青海东北部大道河。
